news_summary: Citizens in Venezuela have taken proactive leadership in relief efforts following major twin earthquakes, demonstrating community resilience and solidarity in the face of disaster.
news_summary_fr: Au Venezuela, les citoyens ont pris l'initiative dans les opérations de secours à la suite de deux séismes majeurs, faisant preuve de résilience et de solidarité communautaires face à la catastrophe.
news_summary_es: Los ciudadanos de Venezuela han asumido un liderazgo proactivo en las labores de socorro tras los dos grandes terremotos que se han producido, demostrando la resiliencia y la solidaridad de la comunidad ante la catástrofe.

articles.title: Press release - EP TODAY
articles.summary: Tuesday 7 July Source : © European Union, 2026 - EP
articles.content:
Air passenger rights

Parliament is set to confirm at noon the first update to the air passenger rights regulation since 2004. Parliament’s negotiating team resisted a push to weaken air passengers’ rights when flights are delayed or cancelled, while securing improvements to the rights of disabled passengers and parents travelling with children. A press conference with Andrey Novakov (EPP, BG), rapporteur, and Virginijus Sinkevičius (Greens/EFA, LT), vice-chair of the Committee on Transport and Tourism is scheduled at 14.00.

Gediminas VILKAS

(+32) 470 89 29 21

@EP_Transport

Irish presidency of the Council

Taoiseach Micheál Martin will present Ireland’s Council presidency priorities to plenary in a debate with Commissioner Maroš Šefčovič starting at 10.30. Under the banner “Strength with Unity”, Ireland is focusing on three policy pillars: competitiveness, values, and security. Reaching agreement on the EU’s long-term budget for 2028 to 2034 will be an overarching priority. A press conference with Micheál Martin and Parliament’s president Roberta Metsola will take place at 13.00.

Eoghan WALSH

(+32) 485 39 94 43

@EuroParlPress

Enlargement: Ukraine, Moldova, Serbia

Three consecutive debates with Enlargement Commissioner Marta Kos on the accession pathways of Ukraine, Moldova and Serbia start at 16.30. First in line is Ukraine, followed by Moldova at 18.30 and ending with Serbia at 19.30. Parliament will vote on its respective reports tomorrow at noon. Press conferences with the standing rapporteurs for Serbia and Moldova will follow the votes.

Snjezana KOBESCAK SMODIS

(+32) 470 96 08 19

@EP_ForeignAff

EU-Mexico partnership

Before voting tomorrow, MEPs and Commissioner Maroš Šefčovič will debate two agreements paving the way for closer political ties and expanded trade between the EU and Mexico. The EU-Mexico modernised global agreement (MGA) will replace the current framework, in place since 2000. The interim trade agreement (iTA), would allow for the updated trade arrangements to apply earlier and expire once the MGA fully enters into force. A press conference with the rapporteurs will take place tomorrow after the votes.

Janis KRASTINS

(+371) 26 54 23 69

@EP Trade

In brief

Automotive sector. MEPs start the day at 9.00 with a debate with Commission executive vice-president Stéphane Séjouné on the need for urgent measures to strengthen the automotive sector’s competitiveness and safeguard quality jobs.

Cybersecurity and AI. MEPs will quiz Commission executive vice-president Henna Virkkunen on the Commission’s brand new cybersecurity and AI action plan in a scrutiny session starting at 15.00.

Social security coordination. An informal agreement on new rules to ensure access to social security for EU citizens living or working in another EU country will be put to a vote at noon. A press conference with rapporteur Gabriele Bischoff (S&D, DE) is scheduled at 15.00.

Fertiliser prices. At noon, Parliament is set to adopt changes to the common agricultural policy rules proposed by the Commission to help farmers affected by the rising costs of fertilisers.

Compliance of ESN party with EU values. Parliament will also vote on whether to request the Authority for European Political Parties and European Political Foundations to verify whether Europe of Sovereign Nations political party complies with EU values.

ePrivacy derogation to detect child sexual abuse. In a vote at noon, MEPs will decide whether to apply the urgent procedure to a proposal to reinstate a derogation to the ePrivacy Directive to allow internet platforms to detect child sexual abuse. If agreed, the vote on the substance of the proposal will take place later this week.

Votes

At noon, MEPs will also vote, among others, on:

mobilisation of the European Union Solidarity Fund for Spain, Romania and Cyprus with regard to natural disasters in 2025;

whether to extend the term of office of the Special Committee on the Housing Crisis in the EU, and adjust its responsibilities;

whether to waive the immunity of Klára Dobrev (S&D, HU);

proposals for a coherent tax framework for the EU's financial sector;

an own-initiative report on digital assets and the challenges they pose for the EU’s financial system;

the 2025 annual report on the financial activities of the European Investment Bank Group;

a recommendation on the changing geopolitical situation in East Asia and the need for closer cooperation with like-minded partners in the region; and

an own-initiative report on a new strategy for media literacy and digital learning.

Live coverage of the plenary session can be found on Parliament’s webstreaming site and on EbS+.

For detailed information on the session, please also see our newsletter.

Find more information regarding plenary.
articles.reasoning: The article reports on the European Parliament updating air passenger rights for the first time since 2004, including stronger protections for disabled passengers and parents traveling with children. This represents a meaningful policy improvement with broad impact on millions of travelers across the EU, enhancing rights and accessibility. The article provides sufficient detail on the legislative focus and related parliamentary activities, indicating a substantive positive change in passenger rights.
articles.personality_title: European Parliament updates air passenger rights for the first time in 18 years
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Since 2004, the rules protecting air passengers in the European Union have remained largely the same. These rules explain what passengers can expect if their flights are delayed or cancelled.

**What happened** – On Tuesday, the European Parliament approved the first major update to these air passenger rights. The new rules strengthen protections for all passengers, especially for disabled travelers and parents flying with children. Lawmakers worked hard to keep these rights strong, rejecting attempts to weaken them.

**Impact** – This update improves travel experiences for millions of people across the EU. Disabled passengers will find it easier to get help and support, while parents will have clearer rights when traveling with their children. Overall, the changes make flying fairer and more accessible.

**What's next step** – The updated rules will now be formally confirmed and put into practice. Airlines and airports will need to follow the new standards, improving services for passengers. The European Parliament will continue to monitor how these changes work in real life.

**One-sentence takeaway** – The European Parliament has strengthened air passenger rights for the first time in nearly two decades, making flying fairer and more supportive for disabled people and families.
articles.personality_title_fr: Le Parlement européen renforce les droits des passagers aériens pour la première fois depuis 18 ans
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Depuis 2004, les règles protégeant les passagers aériens dans l’Union européenne sont restées en grande partie les mêmes. Ces règles expliquent ce que les passagers peuvent attendre en cas de retard ou d’annulation de vol.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Mardi, le Parlement européen a approuvé la première mise à jour majeure de ces droits des passagers aériens. Les nouvelles règles renforcent les protections pour tous les passagers, en particulier pour les personnes en situation de handicap et les parents voyageant avec des enfants. Les députés ont refusé les tentatives d’affaiblir ces droits.

**Impact** – Cette mise à jour améliore l’expérience de voyage de millions de personnes dans l’UE. Les passagers handicapés pourront plus facilement obtenir de l’aide, et les parents auront des droits plus clairs lorsqu’ils voyagent avec leurs enfants. Globalement, ces changements rendent le voyage en avion plus juste et accessible.

**Prochaine étape** – Les nouvelles règles seront désormais confirmées officiellement et mises en œuvre. Les compagnies aériennes et les aéroports devront respecter ces nouvelles normes, améliorant ainsi les services aux passagers. Le Parlement européen continuera de suivre la mise en pratique de ces changements.

**En une phrase** – Le Parlement européen a renforcé les droits des passagers aériens pour la première fois depuis près de vingt ans, rendant le voyage plus juste et plus accessible pour les personnes handicapées et les familles.
articles.personality_title_es: El Parlamento Europeo actualiza los derechos de los pasajeros aéreos por primera vez en 18 años
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Desde 2004, las normas que protegen a los pasajeros aéreos en la Unión Europea se han mantenido casi iguales. Estas reglas explican qué pueden esperar los pasajeros si sus vuelos se retrasan o cancelan.

**Qué pasó** – El martes, el Parlamento Europeo aprobó la primera actualización importante de estos derechos para los pasajeros aéreos. Las nuevas normas fortalecen la protección para todos los viajeros, especialmente para personas con discapacidad y padres que viajan con niños. Los legisladores rechazaron intentos de debilitar estos derechos.

**Impacto** – Esta actualización mejora la experiencia de viaje para millones de personas en la UE. Los pasajeros con discapacidad podrán recibir más ayuda y apoyo, y los padres tendrán derechos más claros cuando viajan con sus hijos. En general, los cambios hacen que volar sea más justo y accesible.

**Próximo paso** – Las nuevas reglas se confirmarán formalmente y se aplicarán. Las aerolíneas y aeropuertos deberán cumplir con los nuevos estándares, mejorando los servicios para los pasajeros. El Parlamento Europeo seguirá supervisando cómo funcionan estos cambios en la práctica.

**Resumen en una frase** – El Parlamento Europeo ha fortalecido los derechos de los pasajeros aéreos por primera vez en casi dos décadas, haciendo que volar sea más justo y accesible para personas con discapacidad y familias.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed painting of a large, sturdy airplane gently cradled by open hands made of soft clouds, symbolizing protection and care for air passengers’ rights, with subtle silhouettes of a wheelchair and a parent holding a child’s hand nestled within the clouds, all rendered in warm, natural tones of blues, whites, and soft earth colors.

articles.title: Citizens lead relief efforts after major twin quakes in Venezuela
articles.summary: Efforts from volunteers and SaR teams had rescued least 6,000 people alive from stricken areas, according to June 30 numbers from the government.
articles.content:
At 6.04 p.m. local time on June 24, 2026, Venezuela experienced its biggest seismic event in a century: a 7.2 magnitude earthquake, followed 39 seconds later by a 7.5 one.

As of July 6, 3,342 deaths had been confirmed, with 16,740 wounded and more than 16,000 left homeless, according to the the country´s Information Ministry. The unofficial tally of people reported missing has reached 41,000, and at least 58,000 buildings suffered significant damage, as shown by satellite imagery released by NASA.

Though their epicenters were located 160 kilometers (just short of 100 miles) away, the “doublet quakes”’ (also experienced in Syria and Turkey in 2023) rattled the heart of the South American country. Caracas, the Venezuelan capital city, and neighboring La Guaira, a coastal state situated 40 minutes away from Caracas by car, next to the Caribbean Sea, bore the brunt of the impact. The country's main international airport and seaport are located in La Guaira, as are many residential and vacation homes. As of 2011, the most recent census completed to date, approximately five million people lived in the combined area.

Venezuela’s northern coast is close to colliding tectonic plates situated in the Caribbean Sea and the South American continent, respectively. The Caracas-La Guaira area has experienced its share of natural disasters, including another earthquake in 1967 and major floods in 1999. Despite this, scientists regard the current Venezuelan seismic infrastructure as practically nonexistent. This time around, though, Android phones alerted many Venezuelans right before the quakes struck.

June 24 was a public holiday in Venezuela, so schools and offices were closed. When the twin earthquakes hit, many people were waiting to watch FIFA World Cup matches or simply spending a calm day with their families. Now, many of them are involved in searching for their relatives or organizing widespread community-led relief efforts, joined by a huge wave of international volunteers. Damages will cost at least USD 6.7 billion (or six percent of Venezuela’s GDP), a preliminary estimate from the United Nations Development Programme.

The event has added to a severe, pre-existing humanitarian crisis, complete with — among other issues that remain unchanged after the January 2026 U.S. intervention — a failing healthcare system and ill-equipped emergency response teams. There has been growing criticism of the “slow” response of the Venezuelan interim government.

Acting President Delcy Rodríguez, who claims the delay is a manufactured narrative from propaganda laboratories, said on July 3, “We did not wait one day, two days or three days. We activated immediately.”

The Rodriguez-led administration has set controversial measures such as mandatory registration in a Caracas office for all disaster zone-bound volunteers, including first-aid personnel, and coverage restrictions for local and foreign press.

Citizens, the first respondents

Local survivors were the first ones to pick up hand tools like axes and even use their bare hands to search for their loved ones, as seen at fallen buildings in La Guaira and Caracas. Others incessantly used Tiktok, Instagram and newly unblocked X to find missing people or post urgent calls for rescue personnel or supplies.

Many kept working hard around collapsed buildings, well beyond what rescue experts call the 72-hour window to find survivors.

Professional search and rescue (SaR) work also continued, undertaken by Venezuelan teams and from June 25, in the most affected areas, by teams from 30 other countries, including Argentina, Chile, Italy, Jordan, Mexico, Qatar, Spain, and the United States. Successful rescue stories — such as the retrieval of Dayana Patiño and her 8-day old baby boy, Juan David, after 32 hours under the rubble — were widely celebrated across international social media.

According to July 3 numbers from the government, efforts from volunteers and SaR teams have resulted in the rescue of least 6,000 people — alive — from stricken areas.

However, at least one episode involving Venezuelan officials marred the efforts of SaR teams. Francisco Lermanda, working with Topos de Chile (a volunteer-led SaR group), denounced incessant requests for identification from local military in active rescue zones in La Guaira. “We have orders to check your documents, because you can be a spy for the Yankees, for the Chileans,” officials reportedly told Lermanda, a seasoned rescue worker.

Kinship beyond borders

After a disaster, technology connects people and acts as a catalyst for recovery. After confirming that his parents were okay in Los Teques, a city close to Caracas, Elías Haig, a Venezuelan contributor to Global Voices, residing in the United States for college studies, set out to do something for his home country.

“I thought, ‘They will need to find missing persons,‘” Haig told Global Voices via WhatsApp. “Setting up a centralized information center, under still ongoing web blockades by the government, was impossible. We were also going to see fakes.”

The student immediately got to work, producing an OSINT-based page with additional AI-based capabilities aimed at compiling confirmed reports about missing people. He took it down on June 25 and forwarded his reports to larger web-based sites, also developed independently.

“I will do whatever adds value, not obstruct anything,” Haig added. He is currently undertaking fundraising efforts with the help of local friends. Other global initiatives, many from the eight million-strong Venezuelan diaspora, are channeling aid to trustworthy non-governmental organizations (NGOs) on the ground, voluntarily providing remote structural evaluation and medical and psychological aid to those affected.

“We must continue”

Multiple volunteer-led relief efforts can physically be seen across Venezuela, in the form of collection points, refuge camps and more.

Caritas, a renowned Catholic-affiliated global non-profit, is one of the leading distribution networks currently channeling aid across affected areas in Venezuela. Many citizens, including residents from outside the most affected areas, have come to the organization’s Caracas headquarters to deliver donations or sign up as volunteers for what experts estimate will be a long-term effort.

Outside the Caritas headquarters, a long line of trucks and small cars stood waiting to deliver donations. One of them was a group from El Tigre — a city located close to oil fields six hours away in eastern Venezuela, which did not suffer major damage.

“We must continue,” said José Rivero, the young driver who, in the space of three days, had voluntarily made three trips back and forth between El Tigre and Caracas, using his family’s truck to bring food, clothing and medicine collected by his church. His words echo the feeling of many in the disaster-stricken nation.
articles.reasoning: The article highlights a significant natural disaster with tragic consequences but focuses on the inspiring large-scale citizen-led relief efforts and international volunteer support that have rescued thousands of people. It emphasizes community resilience, humanitarian aid, and cross-border solidarity, which have broad positive societal impact and provide hope amid crisis.
articles.personality_title: Venezuelan communities and global volunteers rescue thousands after twin earthquakes
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – On June 24, 2026, Venezuela faced two strong earthquakes within 39 seconds, measuring 7.2 and 7.5 on the Richter scale. These quakes caused widespread damage, especially in Caracas and La Guaira, leaving thousands dead, wounded, or homeless.

**What happened** – In the days after the earthquakes, local people immediately began searching for survivors using whatever tools they had, even their bare hands. Volunteers and professional search and rescue teams from Venezuela and 30 other countries joined the effort. By July 3, at least 6,000 people had been rescued alive from the rubble. Technology like social media helped families find missing loved ones and coordinate aid.

**Impact** – The quick action of citizens and international helpers saved many lives despite damaged infrastructure and slow official responses. Stories like rescuing a mother and her newborn after 32 hours under rubble show determination and teamwork. Community groups, churches, and global Venezuelan networks also gathered and shared food, medicine, and shelter, showing strong solidarity.

**What's next step** – Relief efforts will continue for months as many people still need help rebuilding homes and lives. Organizations like Caritas are managing donations and volunteers, while online platforms track missing persons and support fundraising. The cooperation between citizens, international teams, and NGOs will remain vital.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Local courage and worldwide teamwork have saved thousands after Venezuela’s twin earthquakes, turning tragedy into a powerful story of rescue and support.
articles.personality_title_fr: Communautés vénézuéliennes et volontaires internationaux sauvent des milliers de vies après les séismes jumeaux
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le 24 juin 2026, le Venezuela a subi deux puissants séismes en l’espace de 39 secondes, mesurant 7,2 et 7,5 sur l’échelle de Richter. Ces tremblements de terre ont causé d’importants dégâts, notamment à Caracas et La Guaira, laissant des milliers de morts, blessés ou sans abri.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Dans les jours qui ont suivi, les habitants ont immédiatement commencé à chercher des survivants avec les moyens du bord, parfois même à mains nues. Des volontaires et des équipes de recherche et sauvetage professionnelles du Venezuela et de 30 autres pays ont rejoint les opérations. Au 3 juillet, au moins 6 000 personnes ont été sauvées vivantes des décombres. Les réseaux sociaux ont aidé les familles à retrouver leurs proches et à organiser l’aide.

**Impact** – La rapidité d’action des citoyens et des aides internationales a permis de sauver beaucoup de vies malgré des infrastructures endommagées et une réponse officielle jugée lente. Des histoires comme celle d’une mère et de son nouveau-né sauvés après 32 heures sous les décombres témoignent de la détermination et de la solidarité. Des groupes communautaires, des églises et des réseaux vénézuéliens dans le monde ont aussi collecté et partagé nourriture, médicaments et abris.

**Prochaine étape** – Les efforts de secours vont durer plusieurs mois car de nombreuses personnes doivent encore reconstruire leur vie et leur logement. Des organisations comme Caritas gèrent les dons et les volontaires, tandis que des plateformes en ligne suivent les personnes disparues et soutiennent les collectes de fonds. La coopération entre citoyens, équipes internationales et ONG restera essentielle.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Le courage local et la collaboration mondiale ont permis de sauver des milliers de vies après les séismes jumeaux du Venezuela, transformant une tragédie en une histoire forte de sauvetage et de soutien.
articles.personality_title_es: Comunidades venezolanas y voluntarios internacionales rescatan a miles tras los terremotos gemelos
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El 24 de junio de 2026, Venezuela sufrió dos fuertes terremotos en 39 segundos, de magnitudes 7.2 y 7.5 en la escala de Richter. Estos sismos causaron daños graves, especialmente en Caracas y La Guaira, dejando miles de muertos, heridos y personas sin hogar.

**Qué pasó** – Días después, los habitantes comenzaron a buscar sobrevivientes con las herramientas que tenían, incluso con las manos. Equipos voluntarios y profesionales de rescate de Venezuela y 30 países más se unieron a las labores. Para el 3 de julio, al menos 6,000 personas fueron rescatadas con vida. Las redes sociales ayudaron a las familias a encontrar a sus desaparecidos y a coordinar la ayuda.

**Impacto** – La rápida acción de ciudadanos y ayudantes internacionales salvó muchas vidas a pesar de la infraestructura dañada y respuestas oficiales lentas. Historias como la de una madre y su bebé recién nacido rescatados tras 32 horas bajo los escombros muestran determinación y trabajo en equipo. Grupos comunitarios, iglesias y redes venezolanas globales también recolectaron y distribuyeron comida, medicinas y refugio, demostrando solidaridad.

**Próximo paso** – Los esfuerzos de ayuda continuarán por meses, ya que muchas personas necesitan reconstruir sus hogares y vidas. Organizaciones como Caritas gestionan donaciones y voluntarios, mientras plataformas en línea rastrean desaparecidos y apoyan la recaudación de fondos. La cooperación entre ciudadanos, equipos internacionales y ONG seguirá siendo clave.

**Conclusión en una frase** – El valor local y la colaboración global han salvado a miles tras los terremotos gemelos en Venezuela, convirtiendo la tragedia en una historia de rescate y apoyo.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting showing a cluster of hands—some holding simple rescue tools like axes and ropes, others reaching out gently—emerging from stylized rubble shaped like fragmented buildings, with soft glowing lanterns floating above symbolizing hope and community-led relief under a calm twilight sky with faint outlines of Caracas and La Guaira coastlines in the background, all rendered in natural, muted earth tones and soft blues.
